retrieved_docs.title: Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump -- Clinical Significance -- Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump in Acute Myocardial Infarction
retrieved_docs.content: Recently published long-term 6-year follow-up of IABP-SHOCK II Trial revealed no beneficial effect on all-cause mortality. Unfortunately, a higher mortality rate of 2 out of 3 patients with cardiogenic shock is observed despite receiving treatment with revascularization therapy. Moreover, no difference in recurrent MI or repeat revascularization, stroke, or re-hospitalization due to cardiac etiology, quality of life among survival (assessed by EuroQol 5D Questionnaire and New York Heart Association class) was observed. [34] The currently available evidence suggests no short-term or long-term clinical benefit of IABP in patients with post-AMI cardiogenic shock without mechanical defects and endorses the recommendations of European Society of Cardiology to avoid the routine use of the IABP.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify risk factors for acute coronary syndrome. Identify how a patient with acute coronary syndrome might present, and describe the evaluation that should be done. Describe the treatment of acute coronary syndrome. Describe the importance of well-coordinated interprofessional teamwork in caring for patients with acute coronary syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Syndrome X -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Long-term outcomes of CSX may be determined by typical disease characteristics such as chest pain, poor intracoronary microvascular response to acetylcholine (Ach), the severity of endothelial dysfunction, and ischemia of the myocardium. [3] [5] Endothelial dysfunction of the coronary vessel wall and intracoronary microvascular response to Ach are vital predictors of prognosis. Severe dysfunction poses a significant risk for atheroma formation and acute coronary syndrome (ACS). Patients with established CSX often tend to have unfavorable cardiovascular events, leading to recurring hospitalizations, decreased quality of life, and poor prognostic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: In the absence of absolute contraindication, anticoagulation is indicated in all patients with UA/NSTEMI once the diagnosis is made. [11] [55] [71] Enoxaparin, UFH, and fondaparinux are listed as class I of recommendation for this purpose. [11] [55] [67] Enoxaparin and fondaparinux should be avoided in patients with severe renal failure. Data from the SYNERGY trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in terms of efficacy, with the expense of a modest increase in bleeding risk. [72] [73] On the other hand, a meta-analysis published eight years after the SYNERGY trial reported a superior effect of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in reducing mortality and bleeding outcomes during PCI in patients with ACS. [37] The benefit was observed mostly in patients with STEMI. OASIS-5 study has demonstrated non-inferiority in the efficacy of fondaparinux compared to enoxaparin, with a better bleeding risk profile. [74] An important caveat was an increased risk of catheter-related thrombosis during PCI, which was successfully mitigated by intraprocedural UFH administration. [74] [75] Based on these observations, a reasonable approach would be that in patients undergoing early invasive strategy, where PCI is expected, the anticoagulant of choice should be UFH, while enoxaparin could be an option in the absence of bleeding risk and renal failure. Fondaparinux should be considered in patients with higher bleeding risk in the absence of advanced renal failure but would mandate intraprocedural UFH administration to prevent intra-procedural catheter thrombosis. In patients managed by ischemia-guided strategy and PCI is unlikely to happen, fondaparinux offers optimal safety profile, while observations from ESSENCE and TIMI 11B trial suggest that enoxaparin in this clinical scenario should be favored when compared to UFH. [76] [77] Bivalirudin is recommended as an anticoagulant agent in patients with UA/NSTEMI who have heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Otherwise, bivalirudin offers a questionable benefit to a very small group of patients. Those patients would have characteristics that favor a strong inclination to add GpIIb/IIIa inhibitors and, at the same time, would have to be at increased risk of bleeding. In that situation, given non-inferiority in ischemic risk and lower incidence of bleeding with bivalirudin monotherapy compared to LMWH or UFH+GpIIb/IIIa, bivalirudin would be a choice. [68] Anticoagulation in the acute phase of UA/NSTEMI treatment is discontinued after revascularization, or in the case when no revascularization is performed, discontinuation is performed after at least 48 hours. [11] [55] [71]

Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with STEMI who are undergoing fibrinolysis, an anticoagulant is necessary until revascularization occurs. If there is no planned revascularization, it should be administered at least 48 hours, up to 8 days. [12] [7] Trials such as ASSENT 3 and ExTRACT-TIMI 25 demonstrated the benefit of enoxaparin when compared to UFH, although the latter study showed increased non-cerebral bleeding complications in patients treated with enoxaparin. The study also included patients with creatinine clearance less than 30 mL/min and patients older than 75 with appropriate dose adjustment. [40] [41] It seems reasonable to prefer enoxaparin over UFH in patients with STEMI undergoing fibrinolysis, although guidelines suggest class I recommendation for both agents. [12] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Acute coronary syndrome refers to a group of diseases in which blood flow to the heart is decreases. Some examples include ST-elevation myocardial infarction, non-ST elevation myocardial infarction, and unstable angina. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of patients with acute coronary syndrome, and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in caring for these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and chest pain (ألم الصدر) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). This relationship indicates that elevated blood cholesterol levels can contribute to the development of acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and may manifest as chest discomfort. Recognizing hypercholesterolemia as an underlying factor for these cardiac presentations underscores the importance of lipid screening and management in patients who exhibit chest pain or anginal symptoms, thereby aiding in risk stratification and preventive cardiovascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2366
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, Acute Q wave myocardial infarction, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the intermediate artery, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the mid portion of the right coronary artery, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, Acute non‑Q wave infarction, Acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction all share the same anatomic finding site: the myocardium. This indicates that, irrespective of the presence or absence of Q waves, the type of ST‑segment change, the specific coronary artery segment that is occluded, or the clinical context (such as postoperative status or a subsequent event), the primary tissue affected in each of these acute myocardial infarction entities is the heart muscle itself. Recognizing that the myocardium is the common locus of injury across these diverse presentations is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapeutic interventions, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2405
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (including the generic disorder and the specific forms caused by occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and involvement of the left anterior descending coronary artery), acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are all distinct clinical entities that share a common anatomic focus: the myocardium. Each of these conditions represents an acute loss of blood supply to myocardial tissue, whether the injury is precipitated by specific coronary artery branch occlusions, surgical or procedural contexts, or broader patterns of infarction. Recognizing that all of these varied presentations localize to the myocardium underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction can present in many distinct clinical and electrocardiographic forms, yet all of these entities share the common pathological involvement of the myocardium. The condition includes classic presentations such as Acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction and Acute non‑Q‑wave infarction – widespread, as well as various ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) that are defined by the specific coronary artery segment that is occluded. Examples of STEMI subtypes are Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the proximal, distal, marginal, septal, or posterior descending branches of the right coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery. Additional classifications encompass Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also represented, such as Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Postoperative settings can give rise to Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. Despite the diversity of terminology, anatomical location, and timing, each of these acute coronary syndromes is characterized by injury to the myokard (myocardial tissue). Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal site of injury across all these variants is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease, which is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, denotes a category of cardiovascular conditions that are not precisely classified. This category includes the SMQ اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَلب, indicating that myocardial inflammation is considered a member of other and ill-defined heart disease. Recognizing that other and ill-defined heart disease and تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب refer to the same clinical grouping helps clinicians organize and address these ambiguous cardiac disorders within patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, indicating that the broader category includes infarcts defined simply by the occluded major coronary vessel. The classification further extends to Acute myocardial infarction during procedure, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), and Acute widespread myocardial infarction, demonstrating that Acute infarct also captures procedural‑related, non‑ST‑segment elevation, and extensive myocardial injury patterns. Recognizing these diverse entities under the shared morphology of Acute infarct aids clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and selection of targeted therapeutic strategies for patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2300

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2455
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave myocardial infarction, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the intermediate artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the mid portion of the right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery, acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction during a procedure, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, and postoperative acute myocardial infarction are all conditions in which the pathological finding is located in the myocardium (myokard). This consistent association underscores that, regardless of the specific coronary artery involved, the type of infarction, or the clinical setting (e.g., procedural or postoperative), the primary site of injury in these acute myocardial infarctions is the heart muscle itself. Recognizing the myocardium as the common finding site is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted imaging, and appropriate therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving myocardial tissue and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

Together, these entities illustrate that acute infarct encompasses both Q‑wave and non‑Q‑wave patterns and a variety of STEMI presentations determined by the precise location of coronary artery obstruction, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770

This classification aids in the systematic organization of cardiovascular diagnoses and informs clinical assessment and management strategies.
